As an improvement over using a class to store state and to provide a callable interface, a Python function is also callable and can be used for the Retry:

```
def call_mutate_row(table, request_pb):
    """Call the MutateRow"""
    client = table._instance._client
    client._data_stub.MutateRow(request_pb)
```

Using this in a Retry looks like the following:

```
        retry_commit = wrapped_partial(call_mutate_row,
                                       self._table,
                                       request_pb)
        retry_ = retry.Retry(
            predicate=retry.if_exception_type(exceptions.GrpcRendezvous),
            deadline=30)

        retry_(retry_commit)()
```

OK, so to pull the ideas into one place, possibilities include:

1. A per-task option, and you just have to flip this on or off per-task. Advantage - existing flags don't change.

2. A recursive global option, which tasks can ignore if recursiveness isn't relevant to them. A bit awkward. Existing flags don't change.

3. A recursive option at the `lint` goal scope (not a Subsystem, just a recursive registration). This is something we haven't done before, but "should work". It would mean moving all the lint-ish tasks that are currently in `compile` to `lint`. Then the flag is, e.g., `--[no-]lint-errorprone-recursive`, but the `./pants lint.errorprone --recursive` shortcut works. Can't be reused for non-lint tasks. Existing flags must be migrated. Not sure what this does to the help text generator etc.

4. A subsystem, with a scoped instance for each task. Say the subsystem is called 'TargetRange', then the flag is, e.g., `--[no-]-targetrange-lint-errorprone-recursive` etc. Verbose, and there's no shortcut.  Can be used by all tasks for which recursiveness is a relevant concept. Existing flags must be migrated.

That being said @mikebrules, this is where I'm at. I have a script that wraps the cfn package/deploy which takes in any stage related variables i.e STAGE, VPCID, things like that. 

Here it is
```
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -e

if [ -z "$1" ]
  then
    echo "Argument stage is required"
    exit 1
fi

STAGE=$1
SERVICE="some-rando-name"
BUCKET="template-bucket"
REGION="us-west-2"

echo "CloudFormation packaging..."

Hi,

At this moment SAM does not support an existing bucket as an event source. It is mentioned in the documentation:
https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/master/versions/2016-10-31.md#s3

"NOTE: To specify an S3 bucket as an event source for a Lambda function, both resources have to be declared in the same template. AWS SAM does not support specifying an existing bucket as an event source."

Makes sense. We should expand this into a proper documentation file, but here is a start. You can `!Ref` or `!GetAtt` these resources. I understand you would be Referencing a resource that does not exist in the template. A better solution is to create SAM-specific intrinsics that will be resolved by SAM to appropriate resources. But until that happens, you can rely on the resource names:

`AWS::Serverless::Function` creates the following resources:
* `<function-logical-id>Role` is the execution role of function (`AWS::IAM::Role` resource type)
*  API Events:
    * `ServerlessRestApi`: LogicalID of the implicit `AWS::ApiGateway::RestApi` created by SAM.   
    * `ServerlessRestApiProdStage`:  `AWS::ApiGateway::Stage` resource created by SAM for API. If you happen to specify the `RestApiId` property of the event, replace `Prod` with stage name specified by the RestApi.
    * `ServerlessRestApiDeployment<Sha-of-Swagger>`: `AWS::ApiGateway::Deployment` resource for implicit API. The swagger's SHA helps SAM to re-deploy the API every time you update the APIs
    * `<function-logical-id><api-name>Permission<stage-name>`:  Lambda permissions (`AWS::Lambda::Permissions` resource type) required to give API Gateway access to invoke Lambda. <stage-name> is set to Prod for implicit APIs.
* Other events: TBD

`AWS::Serverless::Api` creates the following resources:
* `<api-logical-id>`: AWS::ApiGateway::RestApi resource
* `<api-logical-id><stage-name>Stage`: `AWS::ApiGateway::Stage` resource where <stage-name> is equal to the StageName property you specified. If you happened to use intrinsics here, SAM does not have access to resolve this. <stage-name> would be skipped in this case and become just `<api-logical-id>Stage`
* `<api-logical-id>Deployment<Sha-of-Swagger>`: `AWS::ApiGateway::Deployment` resource for with first 10 characters of SHA of the swagger appended to allow automatic redeployment

`AWS::Serverless::SimpleTable` creates just a `AWS::DynamoDB::Table` resource with the same LogicalId

I will expand this with more examples add to the `README`.

It's nice that we can already reference them for each function, and that solves the issue of hard-coded parameters. But we still have to put the same reference into all Functions (if we use the same Runtime, for example), which in a way goes against the goals of simplifying serverless configuration. An already existing counter-example of that (IMO) is the implied API resource that gets created if we don't specify RestAPI ourselves. I think such convention-over-configuration is good for keeping things concise and simple, as long as we clearly state what the default behaviors are.

I agree that defaults specified as "parameters" may/will lead to conflicts. Maybe it'd be better to create a dedicated section (e.g. 'DefaultProperties'), not related to the 'Parameters' section (if only through references), which would explicitly state which defaults are used throughout the template. This way, there would be only one logical path, at least in how I see it: 'apply DefaultProperties' -> 'apply Properties', where 'by default' the DefaultProperties section is implied to be empty, and the behavior of 'apply Properties' would be to set the final values, overwriting any modifications made in DefaultProperties. Or does the internal logic for parsing properties for each Function act independently of any 'global context' of the template (if there's any)?

The solution would be to introduce `PMMLCountVectorizer` and `PMMLTfidfVectorizer` subclasses, respectively, that automatically fill in "fixed value" arguments, and only query the end user for "variable value" arguments.

The main reasons that I used this approach in spite of my cognisance of the above risks are these:

1) Our use of the loading mutex prevents pageState that has not been set by the current routing action from interfering with initial view rendering. 

2) Our routing actions define the complete state required for view rendering in their pageState dispatch, so anything potential for interference is overwritten. 

The concern of aggregation of state seems more of an aesthetic one than one that will have downstream effects, thanks to the above two reasons. 

That said, I can make a lower risk change, so will look at the possibility of a minimal reversion and fix before release.

I have a use-case question, too: does *anyone* need a loop-per-`WebSocket*` factory, or is the ability to change "the" loop sufficient?

`txaio` is basically a lowest-common-denominator library between asyncio and Twisted -- and since Twisted only has "a" reactor loop currently, so does txaio.

Yes, I agree if renaming ApplicationSession->Component then we should do the PEP8 naming too.

However, I'm not completely sold on the ``Client.start`` as an API: a helper-method style to run is better than introducing a subclass-only API, IMO. It's been a while since I looked at the new-api things, but something like https://github.com/meejah/AutobahnPython/blob/refactor-transport--newapi-examples/autobahn/twisted/wamp.py#L266 (ignoring whether that's actually the API, or actually the newest version of that idea). In that context, ``.open()`` was the entry-point to call ``react()`` on; this basically called ``.start`` on Connection objects, which only callback'd when they were "done, cleanly" and otherwise would errback.

That is, basically provide a 2-layer API: if you use the ``.start/.connect`` or whatever API, you get a Deferred that either errbacks (if something at all went wrong) or callbacks if everything went fine (e.g. usually "never" but if you decide to exit cleanly, that's the route). So this is where I'm a bit unsure: making that method a thing that's suitable to pass to ``react()`` might constrain it from being an API that can be "upgraded" to a future ``.run()`` helper...But, essentially I *believe* the ``.start`` you propose above is basically the same as ``Connection.start`` in many versions of the new-api...so maybe it will be fine :)

Overall, I think any convenience method to pass to ``react`` provided should be a "bare" helper method (and my colour for that bikeshed would be ``autobahn.twisted.wamp.run`` or similar). This separates the concerns (e.g. "start up my event-loop with these Connection(s) please" versus "connect this Connection, and tell my if it ends or has a problem").

I also think it will force a nicer and more-flexible API on Connection/Client (or equivalent) instances (e.g. ``run([client0, client1])`` etc...). When I was thinking about ``Connection``, that to me abstracted "all connection details" about one thing (urls, credentials, options, etc) and in that way it completely makes sense to want to "run" more than one of those...

Ok so our setup is definitely tailored to have metrics pushed to it from any source (user deployed apps or apps we add to the stack ourselves). If you are wanting to collect metrics from your apps all you need to do is encode them using the [Influx line protocol](https://docs.influxdata.com/influxdb/v0.13/write_protocols/line/) and send them to our nsq instance on the metrics topic. We will then pull that data off using telegraf and send it to influx. Now if you do it this way your data will be colocated in the same database as the other metrics we collect.

Another way, if you dont want to have your data in the same database, is just to send the data directly to influx to the database you want. 

The benefit to use nsq/telegraf is that you can change the destination at anytime without having to change your app code. So you may decide to send that data to librato or kafka and all you need to do is reconfigure telegraf and send the data for you.

> If one of your apps has a wildcard domain, you can still add specific subdomains of the same top-level domain (TLD) to any of your other apps. Specific subdomains are evaluated before wildcard domains when routing requests.

I tried vectorizing the rest. It works with: 
```
x_re = np.array(np.squeeze(np.split(np.tile(x, [1, N-m+1]),
                         np.insert(np.arange(m, (N+1)*(N-m+1)+m, step=N+1),
                         np.arange(N-m+1),
                         np.arange(0, (N+1)*(N-m+1), step=N+1)), axis=1)[1::2]))

but its slower in the end because you have to create a NxN matrix or an array with size N^2.

I'll get an expanded comment in there to explain in more detail why that is there.  In short, it is two independent hacks to ensure that the worker processes shut themselves down in a quiet manner during state sync.  First hack is to ensure that `SIGINT` is ignored at the time that the worker processes start up since processes inherit their signal handlers from their parent process.  This is required because the `concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor` doesn't expose an `initializer` callback like the `multiprocessing.ProcessPool` does.  The second is to call that private method, which is what initializes all of the worker processes.  The `ProcessPoolExecutor` class is lazy and doesn't actually spin up the processes until the first batch of work is submitted.  Reading the source for that module, this was a very clean way to trigger that initialization.

OK, that was pretty easy to diagnose. Stan's analysis is correct. The `Resolve.solve` method is being fed the spec `['pandas', 'numpy', 'python 3.4*']`, along with a list of the installed packages. The solver uses the installed list to extract the installed features, but it doesn't add those solvers to the specs.

The fix is, of course, to include the existing packages in the specs. And that's exactly what happens when we do `conda update --all`: `['xz', 'numpy', 'setuptools', 'openssl', 'pytz', 'h5py', 'hdf5', 'readline', 'zlib', 'wheel', 'python-dateutil', 'pandas', 'six', 'pip', 'sqlite', 'python', 'tk', 'python 3.4*']`

So then we need to ask: what should the difference be between `conda update pandas numpy` and `conda update --all`? The quick fix would mean there is *no* difference; *any* package update would result in a `conda update --all`.

In my view, the ideal behavior is this:
--- First, we test to see if `pandas` and `numpy` can be updated to their latest versions *without updating any other packages*. If so, pull the trigger.
--- Second, we effectively do a `conda update --all`, but we change the solver weights to prefer packages closest to the installed versions.

Needless to say that's a bit more work. But it's doable without a full solver rewrite.

Something else that Ilan pointed out to me is that conda being in the root environment makes it easy to keep conda up to date (because we just inject it into the dependencies).
This is solved by `conda clean --packages` and then `conda update conda`.

Several posts in this thread mentioned the Python error message
```
ImportError: DLL load failed: The specified module could not be found.
```
Unfortunately, Python does not tell us exactly which DLL causes the problem, and it is usually not the module to be imported, but one of its dependencies. In case of png, the missing DLL could be zlib, or the linker attempts to load the wrong version of an existing DLL because two versions happen to be in the PATH, and the wrong one gets preference. If you see the above error message, **always** use [Dependency Walker](http://www.dependencywalker.com/) to find out which DLL is actually causing problems. When invoking Dependency Walker, make sure that the current PATH settings exactly correspond to the ones during the failed import.

I am working on this, yes. It's not trivial, because it requires fixing the performance of the dependency resolution algorithm (see the https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1334).

> Since aarch64 systems are compatible with armhf binaries

This is not in general true; there are aarch64 systems in the field based on the Cavium ThunderX hardware which do not run 32-bit ARM binaries, and these systems are available from Packet ("Type 2A" 96 core) and Scaleway (virtualized 2, 4, or 8 core) in existing configurations.
I'm guessing `conda update conda` should fix your problem.

>  Would it be possible to set the priority for some channels to an equal value

We're continuing to refine this behavior, including intelligent per-package channel pinning.  I like your suggestion too @janschulz.  I think that was always part of the plan actually.

@joachimwolff I've used a slightly different approach. I can't speak as to the efficiency re: your method, but here it is: I use the `pysam.AlignedSegment.tostring()` method to convert the read into a string, which can then safely pass the process boundary. On the other side I use a fromstring method (that I wrote - pysam does not supply one, interestingly) to retrieve the read object.

In my use case, however, the only time reads have to cross process boundaries is when they are part of a read pair where a mate is very far away, so the number of such crossings is relatively low.

> Right now I'm leaning towards saying "cockroachdb doesn't support nested transactions inside other nested transactions" and punt on the nesting depth issue until the database supports depth of greater than 2 (ie., the outer transaction and the nested transaction therein.)

We definitely don't support more than 1 nested transaction. My point is that with your version of `run_transaction`, if you forget to call `commit`, then you've left your connection in a weird state and the next thing you do will potentially get tied into this still-open transaction (or maybe it'll just get a confusing error message when it tries to start another nested layer). 

> If you mean "rollback unless commit() / rollback() was explicitly called", I think that's the default behaviour. However, I'd prefer an exception in that case

Raising an exception (after rolling back to put the connection back in a clean state!) would be fine too (but you're not doing it here).

> the benefit of being able to know that the transaction was successfully committed from within the callback and log or branch on that fact.

Why does it matter that you know that inside the callback instead of in the function that called `run_transaction`? 

In a clean-slate application, I'd argue that the design in which `run_transaction` is responsible for committing the transaction is superior because it is less error-prone. However, I can see that since most existing sqlalchemy code is built around explicit commits, an interface like this can be useful (as a transitional strategy if nothing else), so I could see offering it as an alternative. Now for the tricky part: how to name it to convey the difference from regular `run_transaction`?

That's an interesting use of `epoch`.  It was included primarily for testing.  It was always intended that Curator use time relative to the time of execution to determine which indices are actionable, rather than absolute time.  This is evident that even with the `epoch` option, you are still obligated to use those relative timings, e.g. older than 1 second.

In any case, this feature described in this ticket should otherwise be added to #697

There is a `az network dns record-set {type} delete` command for each record type just to keep the commands in a logical grouping.

receiver.py
```
from azure.servicebus import ServiceBusService, Message
from datetime import datetime
from time import sleep

import config

service = ServiceBusService(
    service_namespace=config.ns_name,
    shared_access_key_name=config.key_name,
    shared_access_key_value=config.key_value,
)

service.create_queue(config.queue_name)

while True:
    message = service.receive_queue_message(config.queue_name, peek_lock=True, timeout=60)
    if message is not None:
        print(message.body + ' received at {0}'.format(datetime.now()))
        message.delete()
    else:
        print('<no message> at {0}'.format(datetime.now()))

```

The adding of a keypair to configuration does not upload a certificate.  It simply makes a reference to an uploaded service certificate.  Your code looks alright but you will need to add the service certificate to the service before deploying this.  The function to do this is sms.add_service_certificate.

Here is some code using this as well as the openssl commands to create a cert.  I upload .pem certs, but pfx should also work.  It is a known oddity that the cert format should be left as 'pfx' even when uploading 'pem' certs.

`
"""Adds the specified SSH Certificate to the specified service_name."""
# if you need to create the key/pem here is a sample
#openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 -keyout filename.key -out filename.pem
#openssl pkcs12 -export -in filename.pem -inkey filename.key -out filename.pfx

with open(cert_path, "rb") as bfile:
	cert_data = base64.b64encode(bfile.read()).decode() # decode to make sure this is a str and not a bstr
	cert_format = 'pfx'
	cert_password = ''
	cert_res = sms.add_service_certificate(service_name=service_name,
						data=cert_data,
						certificate_format=cert_format,
						password=cert_password)
	wait_for_async(sms, cert_res.request_id, "Adding Service Management Cert", 30)
`

The OpenGL renderer shows the collision shapes, which is the convex hull of the STL file. The TinyRenderer takes the original triangle mesh. This is not due to rendering performance reasons. If you want to use a visual shape, it is best to use OBJ files. It is a bit complicated: by default, Bullet will take the convex hull of shapes. If you want to have concave shapes, you need to apply convex decomposition (breaking the concave shape into a collection of convex shape). You can use VHACD for this, in the Bullet/Extras folder.

There are a few differences.

Firstly, Debian Jessie includes certificates that have been deprecated by the CA/Browser forum because their keys are weak (1024-bit). Those certificates are removed from certifi by default (though available on request). These are necessary because Jessie's OpenSSL is old.

Secondly, not all users are on Debian, and so their certificates may not always in the same place or be the same. Certifi ensures that you can always find them, and that they're always the same. The world is bigger than just Debian.

Thirdly, and most importantly, Debian *does not properly process the Mozilla certificate data bundle*. In particular, the Mozilla certificate data bundle marks some certificates as `CKT_NSS_MUST_VERIFY_TRUST` for the purposes of TLS. These certificates are **not trusted for use with securing TLS connections**, instead only safe for S/MIME or code signing. However, Debian does not persist that trust property through to their bundle correctly (as tracked in [this Debian bug](https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=721976) and originally reported in [this Ubuntu bug](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ca-certificates/+bug/1207004)). This means that using the Debian trust bundle leads you to incorrectly trust root certificates you should not be trusting.

Certifi *does* correctly process this field and ships a trust bundle that is suitable *only* for TLS.

I am actually not sure if there is a super good way to do this, because kernels can have different names. Python kernels, regardless of whether they are Python 2 or Python 3, have language "python" but have different display names. So you can't tell based on the language name alone, but I also don't want to enforce that the display name is the same because this might be different for different people (e.g. "IPython 3" vs. "Python 3").

I think issue here is that the frontend doesn't expect its contents to have changed on disk.  What I would have expected is that when it tries to auto-save it would see that the file on disk is different and give you the option to choose what is in the front end or merge.  It could be that the post save hook is not modifying the timestamp by enough time.  A way to test this fact would be to add a delay to the post save hook.  

Another thing to check is whether it works in the classic notebook.  From reading the autosave [source](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/e1ba0075f6c5d901f67918e41f434b1788d592f8/notebook/static/notebook/js/notebook.js#L2661) I'd expect the same friction between a post save hook on the server and the front end data model.

Threaded output is associated with the most recent execution (by the kernel). With a GUI backend, I see the output in the cell (whichever is the most recently executed). With the nbagg backend, the most recent request is likely to be a comm message, and the nbagg backend is presumably dropping those messages. The nbagg backend could hook up the cell output callback for output messages it receives for its comms.

This is something have have played with. In general we have found that most
explicit scrolling events end up causing unpleasant side effects that are
worse than the original problem.

In this case, the input area of cells can have arbitrary length - it very
may be that the scroll cell to top action could scroll the output off the
screen. To get this to work well, we would need some more specific logic
that uses heuristics to try and make good decisions. I am not convinced
those heuristics would work well, but if you are able to make progress, let
us know.

From http://www.json.org/
In JSON, they take on these forms:
An object is an unordered set of name/value pairs.
An array is an ordered collection of values.

In my work I have faced with REST responses  with json data as array on top level

Schematics is designed to apply defaults to those fields that are not present in the input data. If `None` is equated with "not there", it would then become impossible to explicitly initialize a field to `None`. The field would always end up with the default value instead, with no way to override it.

Any suggestions for how to do this? I have a sqlalchemy.JSON field that is a nested dict with a somewhat complicated structure. I don't care about validating the data besides being sure its a valid dict. Is a custom type the right thing to do?

You should look at the CMake scripts used in SimpleITK:

https://github.com/SimpleITK/SimpleITK/blob/master/CMake/sitkSourceVersion.cmake
https://github.com/SimpleITK/SimpleITK/blob/master/CMake/sitkSourceVersionVars.cmake.in

These inspect the SimpleITK repo to extract out this components along with the number of commits since the version file was modified. It also compare the tag version with the version in the version file to determine if the repo is "post" or "pre" release. This enables the release branch to be post, and the master branch to be pre. 

The current version of the SimpleITK master branch is "1.0.0.dev66", the release branch is "0.10.0.post3". There is the optional "+g#####" which is removed when SimpleITK_BUILD_DISTRIBUTE is defined; presumably defined only when on the official master or release branch.

The <number of commits> since version file was modified was chosen over the date because the number of commit is unique for the master/release branch, while there could be multiple commits on the same date. So with the SimpleITK version scheme the version is unique along the release/master branch without the `+g####`, while the above proposal requires it.

These CMake version file should be reusable in both ITK and SimpleITK.

# In general

I think it's good to create a list of issues of stuff that we want to do but can't currently do with the reactive framework. Ideally, we'll find a single solution that allows us to do all these things **with** the reactive framework instead of **in** the reactive framework.

It's preferable to keep the reactive framework as lean as possible and to enable charm developers to put such functionality in a layer.

# Why can't this be done currently?

One way to do this is to have a `config.checked` flag. Each handler that uses the config has the `@when('config.checked)` condition. You create a handler that sets the `config.checked` flag after it checked the config.

The issue with this approach is that the `config.checked` flag will still be enabled when the config changes in a subsequent run.

# How can we solve this?

As a strawman, I'll use the idea of linked states from issue https://github.com/juju-solutions/charms.reactive/issues/97

```python
# Every time `config.changed` gets set, `config.checked` gets removed immediately; before
# running the next handler
reactive.if_this_then_remove('config.changed', 'config.checked')

@when("config.changed")
@when_not("config.checked")
def  check_config():
    if is_valid_config() :
         set_flag('config.checked')

@when(
    "config.checked",
    "config.changed.port",
)
def set_port():
    # ...
```

Clean solution that enables developers to create their own check logic. This has a few advantages:

 - Charmers can make their checking logic as complex as they want
 - Charmers can put checking logic in a layer, for example a layer that checks if certain config options are valid json. You specify which config options should be checked using layer options.
- Charmers can build on top of existing "checking" layers to implement more thorough checking logic. Example: One layer checks if config value is valid json; another layer checks if the json objects have the schema that you want.

And most importantly: **We can be lazy without being a blocker for charm development.**

Over the years, I've written deprecate decorators of the form:

```python
import functools


def deprecate(warning, logger=None):

    def wrap(f):
        _call_count = 0

        @functools.wraps(f)
        def wrapped_f(*args, **kwargs):
            nonlocal _call_count
            if not _call_count:
                # log or print deprecation here
                print(warning)
                _call_count = 1
            return f(*args, **kwargs)
        return wrapped_f
    return wrap


@deprecate("Don't use this")
def deprecated_function():
    # do something
    print("deprecated_function")


@deprecate("This one either")
def f2():
    print("f2 away")


deprecated_function()
deprecated_function()
f2()
deprecated_function()
f2()
f2()
```

with the corresponding output:

    python3 test-deprecate.py
    Don't use this
    deprecated_function
    deprecated_function
    This one either
    f2 away
    deprecated_function
    f2 away
    f2 away


This has the advantage where the deprecated function will only log once, regardless of how many calls there are.  It's then fairly easy to add a date to be removed and just grep the source to see when to remove it.

@abalakh Katello definitely has some issues with it's API. I just found that any OneToOne relationship with organization omits results['organization_id'] by default and only provides results['organization']['id']. #smh So, it seems you can't rely on anything; however, I can tell you how it *should* be:

A OneToOne field should absolutely always have the associated object's ID (i.e. sync_plan_id) at the top level, and there should be no guarantee the associated object's details are resolved and shown along with the top level object's response (i.e. results['sync_plan']['...']). If this were consistent throughout the app, `_get_entity_id` would always grab results['sync_plan_id'] and never need to grab results['sync_plan']['id'].

I don't recommend changing NailGun to how Katello *should* be and coding individual workarounds for inconsistencies, hoping they will eventually get fixed. Instead, we should make NailGun work with the current Katello. I think there are currently 4ish possible API responses for OneToOne relationships (let's take product->sync_plan for example):

```python
'sync_plan_id' in results and 'sync_plan' not in results # 1
'sync_plan_id' in results and 'id' not in results['sync_plan'] # 2
'sync_plan_id' in results and 'id' in results['sync_plan'] # 3
'sync_plan_id' not in results and 'id' in results['sync_plan'] # 4
'sync_plan_id' not in results and 'id' not in results['sync_plan'] # 5 (very unlikely)
```

We can safely assume wherever the ID is, it's correct, and if it's in 2 places, they match.

Currently, NailGun handles 1,3,4; this PR adds support for 2. Obviously, 5 would be a huge bug in Katello that would require immediate fix. :stuck_out_tongue_closed_eyes:
Just curious whether we will need to add more fields in future or it is full set

A common practice I saw in some projects, is having the version in the package root `__init__.py` file. Like requests package [1], but it have to run some logic [2] to get the version number without importing the package, this is important because importing the package may raise an exception if some dependency is not already installed (like in a new environment). Django does the same thing [3] and it ensures that the version is PEP 386 [4] compliant.

I think we should consider those points because the projects are around for a good time and many developers have touched them. With that said, I think having the version in the `__version__` and being a PEP 386 compliant is a good way to go.

[1] https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/requests/__init__.py#L45

[2] https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/setup.py#L31

[3] https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/__init__.py#L5

[4] https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0386/

@pganssle has this issue been assigned or can I take a crack at it?

On second thought, according to [this comment](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/8d1d7e6816753248768e4cc1c0370221814e9cf1/Lib/datetime.py#L1954) (which, frankly, I'm having a bit of trouble following, since I'm not really sure why the notation makes a distinction between `y.n` and `x.n`), overriding `fromutc()` is generally unnecessary, and looking at this more closely, I see what the problem is.

This relates to the fact that `pytz` eagerly evaluates the DST status of the timezone, which allows them to handle ambiguous date representations for the user. In a sense they are extending `datetime` using `tzinfo` objects. This allows them to correctly handle DST status of "nominally" ambiguous times (e.g. times where, in the specified zone, the date represented by `dt.replace(tzinfo=None)` is ambiguous), but causes problems with datetime arithmetic, since they don't re-evaluate the DST status in response to changes in the datetime.

`dateutil` on the other hand, lazily evaluates the time zone as necessary, and as such our timezones handle arithmetic fine, but we can't handle "nominally" ambiguous times, because each time we calculate the DST status, we're doing it 100% based on the (ambiguous) naive datetime object.

This will be solved when [PEP495](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0495/) is implemented and released, because at that point, `datetime` objects will no longer be underspecified with respect to daylight savings.

I see two possible paths forward:

1. Do nothing, because time zones probably shouldn't be stateful in the first place, and wait for PEP495 to solve things for us.
2. Implement as a stop-gap measure a stateful timezone object that (as lightly as possible) overrides `fromutc()`, to allow us to store a "fold" flag in our `tzinfo` classes. This would not work perfectly with arithmetic, but I'm thinking that the "best approximation" would look like this:
    * When `fromutc()` is called, check what side of the DST line we're on and set a `_fold` property appropriately in the `tzinfo` object.
    * When `utcoffset()` (or `tzname()` or anything else requiring that we resolve the actual time zone) is called, check to see if we're at an ambiguous time, then:
        * If we are, resolve in favor of the direction indicated by the `_fold` property.
        * If we're not, set the `_fold` property to whichever side of the fold we're on.
  That will still give some incorrect results when you're applying arithmetic operations and landing in ambiguous times, but I think that that's just something that can't be helped, and this will work fine for most cases.

I'm thinking that there's nothing horribly pathological about the second one. Maybe @abalkin would be willing to comment?

> Any thoughts on how to address the more general issue where validators that make sense for the sqlalchemy implementation type (e.g. CHAR) don't make sense for the python type (e.g. UUID)?

Very good point @andrewmains12 . One approach would be to remove automatic addition of validators and leave it up to the user to opt in to validators.

I prefer to keep the automatic validators because they meet the "90% use case". Special-casing UUID in this case is justified because marshmallow-sqlalchemy has built-in support for the UUID sqlalchemy type.

So I think the best approach for handling other types is to let the user opt out of the automatically-added validators, which they can do using `field_for`.

```python
        full_name = field_for(models.Student, 'full_name', validate=[])
```

The problem is of course not with the serial. This strange behavior was coming from the leds of the arduino, that flash quite rapidly when a string is being send. But the stream, once received, have to be processed real time by an interrupt, like a stepper in CNC. While there was large string passing through the serial, the process time of position computing was about the same as the serial write/read time. But as the serial diminish, the compute time stays the same: That's why it seemed to process slowly...

**Multiple views:**  Sten, I pick up two distinct usage approaches in your response: 
1. a loom file should be used to store a single "branch" of results (i.e. focus on a particular normalization, specific clustering scheme, specific embedding); or 
2. a loom file could contain multiple result branches (e.g. alternative clustering results, derived from different treatments etc.). 
Which one do you think we should strive for here?

On the idea of using naming convention (e.g. `ClusterID_{something}`): I think this would work well for marking the method by which the clustering was determined, but is probably too relaxed for referencing the source data. If loom is storing multiple layers of transformed or normalized data matrices, these have explicit paths to them. I think it would be better if we could reference data path(s) in association with results such as clustering or embeddings. Not sure how to do this easily in hdf5.

> fs.base: setbinfile, setfile and getfile are really confusing: getfile and setbinfile require a binary file, setfile a text file, but has not getfile equivalent. You may be right. I'd have to review that and refresh my memory. Backwards incompatible would not be a deal breaker, as long as we bump the major version number.

> fs.move: move_dir could be implemented using a walker instead of copy_dir, to remove each file after it is moved instead of moving everything. This would be less storage-hungry, and easier to interrupt / resume transfers.

A possible benefit of doing a copy + delete is that if it is interrupted for any reason, you still have all the original files. Since filesystems don't guarantee anything is written until closed, that might be a compelling reason to keep it this way. `shutil.move` (https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/shutil.py#L567) is implemented as a copy + delete of the whole dir, but I'm not sure if that was intentional or just the simplest implementation. Open for debate though...

> fs.multifs: instead of using a namedtuple, we could implement a proper _PrioritizedFS class, and make it comparable. Why would we do that ?... so that we could use the heapq module and a priority queue instead of sorting the delegate filesystems over and over

I think there is a mechanism in there to avoid repeated sorting. It should only sort when it first iterates over the filesystems, then the order will be cached.

def remove_namespaces(self, rpc_reply):
     """remove xmlns attributes from rpc reply"""
     logger.debug('removing namespaces')
     logger.debug(repr(str(rpc_reply)))
     self.__xslt = self.__transform_reply
     self.__parser = etree.XMLParser(remove_blank_text=True)
     self.__parser_nb = etree.XMLParser(remove_blank_text=False)
     self.__xslt_doc = etree.parse(io.BytesIO(self.__xslt), self.__parser)
     self.__xslt_doc2 = etree.parse(io.BytesIO(self.__xslt), self.__parser_nb)
     self.__transform = etree.XSLT(self.__xslt_doc)
     self.__transform2 = etree.XSLT(self.__xslt_doc2)
     self.__root = etree.fromstring(str(self.__transform(etree.parse(StringIO(str(rpc_reply))))))
     self.__root2 = etree.fromstring(str(self.__transform2(etree.parse(StringIO(str(rpc_reply))))))
     logger.debug('removing blank text')
     logger.debug(etree.tostring(self.__root))
     logger.debug('without removing blank text')
     logger.debug(etree.tostring(self.__root2))
     return self.__root

@hamishwillee 

`status_printer` is a method of signature `def status_printer(txt)` which is used to print STATUS_TEXT sent to the vehicle [as well as other diagnostic information—note, not sure if I want to stick with this interpretation, so maybe we just say a STATUS_TEXT printer]. It defaults to `lambda text: sys.stderr.write('>>> ' + text + '\n')`. Passing `None` attaches no listener to STATUS_TEXT. You can pass any method you want to do your printing or logging. (Note: maybe this should be a logging method by default?)

`vehicle_class` defaults to Vehicle. It refers to the class that will be instantiated from the class mechanism. You can change it to any subclass of Vehicle. See #437.

`rate` is the refresh rate sent to `request_data_stream_send` ultimately. A (default) value of 4 means 4Hz updates. 10Hz means ten updates per second. etc...

@hamishwillee 

ArduCopter has several controllers.  

One is the position controller, which goto uses.  This is not appropriate for many of our shots.  

Another one is the velocity controller, which takes input velocities.  And finally, we have the pos-vel controller, which takes both positions and velocities.  We would need Dronekit to expose these in order for the shots to use them.  So I could imagine DroneKit functions like:
setVel() - for velocity controller
setPosVel() - for pos-vel controller

And then in the future:
setAccel() - for acceleration controller

(There are plenty of other things Dronekit could do to better serve the shots code; but we have just not been able to focus on them yet.)

The code from the PR, just in case that PRs on that repo won't be accessible in the future (we may close them completely if people keep sending PRs there instead of here):

    def wavedec_coeff_len(data_len, filter_len, mode, level=None):
        """
        Total number of coefficients in the output of `wavedec`, given data
        length, filter length, mode and level.
        Returns integer: len(cAn) + len(cDn) + len(cDn-1) + ... + len(cD1)
        data_len   - data length
        filter_len - filter length or Wavelet instance
        level      - decomposition level. If level is None then it will be
                     calculated using `dwt_max_level` function.
        """

        if level is None:
            level = dwt_max_level(data_len, filter_len)
        elif level < 0:
            raise ValueError(
                "Level value of %d is too low . Minimum level is 0." % level)
        total_len = 0

        for i in xrange(level):
            data_len = dwt_coeff_len(data_len, filter_len, mode)
            total_len += data_len 

        return total_len + data_len

Some preliminary thoughts:

- having a featurize_many() function that includes a parallelization option sounds good to me. That way, you can process multiple entries without going through a dataframe
- I think we can drop Python 2 compatibility; pymatgen has already stopped requiring new features to be Py2 compatible. We can just be a little more aggressive about Py3 in the message here: https://hackingmaterials.github.io/matminer/installation.html
- Agree that we should only create the Pool if there is >1 worker
- Only thing I am unsure of is whether the default n_jobs should be 1. My initial thought is to have it parallelize by default. If one is running a Jupyter notebook or really doesn't want to parallelize, they should be able to run a line of code like "MMConfig().AUTO_PARALLELIZE = False" which would set the default to False. e.g. you could run that line of code at the top of a Jupyter notebook and then all subsequent calls to featurize_many() would not parallelize without having to set n_jobs each time. Note that I am flexible on the actual form of how it's achieved, but some kind of global variable that can be set once to turn off parallelization and that can be put in the top of Jupyter notebooks. We could also do the inverse - i.e., the default being parallelize=False, but being able to run one line of code near the top that just always turns it on without having to set n_jobs each time.

Another note is that Alex's commit includes many new features, not just parallelization, e.g. the MultiIndex. So we should figure out how to merge all this stuff together.

The 0..1 vs -1..1 should not matter, because first layer of mlp should be able to learn the correct transformation of inputs (unless you explicitly tell it not to use bias, in which case 0..1 and -1..1 are not equivalent). That being said, if the range is very different from 0..1 (say, 0..255, like in atari games), a normalization is needed.  Also (mainly mentioning it for completeness of the picture), there is an auto-normalizing wrapper class (common.vec_env.vec_normalize.VecNormalize) which keeps track of running mean and standard deviation of observations; but it is a bit tricky to serialize (save/load) models with it. Hope this helps and closing this  - feel free to reopen if it does not quite answer your question.

@emilyzzz I solved it by passing `context_value` to `client.execute` explicitly.

First I create fixtures for requests. It's necessary to run them through all required middlewares:

```python
# rf fixture is from django-pytest and make_user is own user model factory

@pytest.fixture
def rq_authenticated(rf, make_user):
    user = make_user(email='test@example.lan', password='test')

    request = rf.post('/')
    SessionMiddleware().process_request(request)
    request.session.save()
    request.user = user

    return request

@pytest.fixture
def rq_anonymous(rf):
    request = rf.post('/')
    SessionMiddleware().process_request(request)
    request.session.save()
    request.user = AnonymousUser()

    return request
```

Test is something like:

```python
def test_query(client, rq_authenticated):
    executed = client.execute(query, context_value=rq_authenticated)
```

I use `rq_authenticated` and `rq_anonymous` explicitly everywhere I check authentication (which is almost everywhere)

Unfortunately, zooming in won't deal with the problem of outliers. The actual summary data that we store has 30 buckets, whose extent spans the extent of your data—all of it. Outliers in your data will cause the interesting data to be clumped together in one bucket. This happens at TensorFlow summary-writing time; there's nothing that the frontend can do to recover that data. You'll need to preprocess your data to remove outliers before passing it to `tf.summary.histogram`. (You may want to do this at different η-thresholds, etc.)

I've tried using the [pyBarcode library](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pyBarcode/0.7) to generate barcode images for python-escpos

Printing "1234" in CODE39 showed a few bugs and missing features. Most of it's in the bug tracker, see [bitbucket](https://bitbucket.org/whitie/python-barcode).

### Example
Dependencies for this snippet:

```bash
pip install python-escpos pyBarcode
```

Hacky setup to get a PIL object out without writing to disk:

```python
import barcode
from barcode.writer import ImageWriter

def get_barcode_image(codetype, content):
    """
    Get a PIL image object from a given barcode type and content.
    
    Note that barcode.PROVIDED_BARCODES contains the list of
    valid barcode types.
    """
    barcode_class=barcode.get_barcode_class(codetype)
    my_code=barcode_class(content, writer=ImageWriter())
    my_code.write("/dev/null")
    return my_code.writer._image
```

The idea being that you could print it:

```python
from escpos.printer import File
a =  File("/dev/usb/lp0")
img = get_barcode_image("code39", "1234")
```

Unfortunately, no chance to set the size, and the output is buggy as anything. Debug time:

```python
img.save(open("aa.png", "wb"), format="png")
```

![aa](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2080552/18787754/86802d66-81e7-11e6-8adc-dc9cf02390ac.png)

### Next up

In the absence of any other suggestions for getting barcodes on the ZJ5890, I think we can close this issue off.

For anyone dealing with a printer which does not support `GS k` correctly, your choices are basically to try a different printer, or to take this snippet as a starting point, looking up issues in [the python-barcode issue tracker](https://bitbucket.org/whitie/python-barcode/issues?status=new&status=open) as you go.

Hi, as you can see from the code the images downloaded and used for your training are indeed normalized to [0.0, 1.0].
```python
import tflearn.datasets.oxflower17 as oxflower17
X, Y = oxflower17.load_data(one_hot=True, resize_pics=(227, 227))
X.min()
[Out] 0.0
X.max()
[Out] 1.0
```
So if you use the exact images for testing you should be good. However if you load another image of your own you have to make sure its pixel values are in the same range as in your training. By the way due to my own experiences it is always best to normalize the values to the range [0.0, 1.0] when training a neural net, regardless of the concrete application.
 
To normalize your images in testing there are several ways, however the most simple way for images is to do
```python
J = J / 255.0
```
Please be aware that this only holds true for uint8 images with a value range of [0, 255].

Another personal oppinion. You shouldn't use scipy.misc.imresize since it is known to change the value range of the image (due to interpolation) which may have a large effect on the overall features that are helpfull to classify an image. It is mentioned here scipy/scipy#4458

So here is a short snippet how it can be done using Pillow and numpy:
```python
from PIL import Image
import numpy as np

J = Image.open('image.png')
J = J.resize([227,227])
J = np.asarray(J).astype(float)
J /= 255.0
```
After this you could try to feed it into your classifier.

Ok I came up with this model definition:

```python
def rnn_conv_lstm_model(width, height, sequence_length):`
    network = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, sequence_length, height, width, 1])
    network = tflearn.reshape(network, [-1, height, width, 1])`
    network = tflearn.conv_2d(network, 32, 3, activation='relu')
    network = tflearn.max_pool_2d(network, 2)
    network = tflearn.conv_2d(network, 64, 3, activation='relu')
    network = tflearn.conv_2d(network, 64, 3, activation='relu')
    network = tflearn.max_pool_2d(network, 2)
    network = tflearn.reshape(network, [-1, sequence_length, (height/2**2)*(width/2**2)*64])
    network = tflearn.lstm(network, 512)
    network = tflearn.fully_connected(network, 2, activation='softmax')
    network = tflearn.regression(network, optimizer='adam', loss='categorical_crossentropy', learning_rate=0.001, name="output1")
    return network
```

Hi @mino98,

Yes, you are correct: word n-grams are only used in supervised mode.

The reason is that when training unsupervised models on large amount of data, the number of n-grams is extremely large, and most of them are not informative (e.g. all the bigrams of the type `the *`). Thus, using word n-grams does not significantly improves the quality of learned models.

One way to address this issue is to only consider "informative" n-grams, such as `New York City`. This can be done by only keeping n-grams made of words with high mutual information. This technique is described in Section 4 of the paper [Distributed Representations of Words and Phrases
and their Compositionality](http://papers.nips.cc/paper/5021-distributed-representations-of-words-and-phrases-and-their-compositionality.pdf), and we used it to train our latest English word representations (see section 2.3 of the paper [Advances in Pre-Training Distributed Word Representations](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1712.09405.pdf) for more information). In particular, we replaced high mutual information phrases such as `New York City` by `New_York_City` with a probability of 0.5 of the time in the training data. We are thinking about making this part of the fastText tool.

Please re-open this issue if you have additional questions!

Best,
Edouard

I've only had a quick look, so there may be other issues, but the main thing is that your class is not picklable. `concurrent` uses Python's `multiprocessing` module which passes arguments between processes by pickling and unpickling them. See the [Pickling support](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/latest/advanced.html#pickling-support) docs to add the required methods to your class.

As for why it works when you limit virtual memory: My guess is that `concurrent`/`multiprocessing` falls back to a single process which does not require pickling.

However, do note that `multiprocessing` is extremely inefficient. It spawns new processes in order to bypass Python's GIL. You'll never be able to get the ideal memory usage with it, but that's not really a pybind11 issue.

You don't have to go through `buffer_info` for this: `array` and `array_t` have a bunch of different constructors taking shapes and strides.

The simplest version, to create a new array that allocates contiguous memory storage, is to use:

```C++
py::array_t<double> arr({ 3, 5 }); // creates a 3x5 matrix with C-style contiguous storage
```

However something you'll quickly run into is that a lot of libraries actually expect and use column-major storage (numpy calls this "Fortran style"), while numpy defaults to row-major (AKA "C-style").  (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Row-_and_column-major_order).  If you need column-major, you can use:

```C++
py::array_t<double, py::array::f_style> arr({ 3, 5 });
```

For ordinary contiguous arrays you don't need to worry about the strides: `py::array` will set those up for you properly for contiguous storage when you create the array.  The only case you'd need to worry about specifying them is where you have non-contiguous storage.

There are various other examples of different ways to construct arrays in the test suite (tests/test_numpy_array.cpp).

Boulder originally allowed JWK to be in the unprotected header, because tampering with the JWK would just result in a failure to validate. It's a bit strange that the code which originally generates the key, in https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/crypto_util.py#L65, already does the right thing (it uses open mode 0600, which is perfectly appropriate for private keys), and there is even a `safe_open` function which supports such modes, but it is not used when the keys are actually saved to the archive directory...

In my opinion, 0600 is the right default mode for any private key material, and you should really use that.  If e.g. Debian or another distro wants to introduce separate 'ssl-cert' groups with additional permissions, then they can patch their copy of certbot while packaging.  I don't think it is up to upstream to cope with all the distro-specific variations.

             logger.debug("Writing symlink to old private key, %s.", old_privkey)

I am sure that you're not linking to the correct SCIP library. When you're using the old Makefile system you end up with several libs: libscip, liblpi, libnlpi, libobjscip, ... With the new CMake system you wil only get one lib that contains everything so linking is a lot easier. This new lib is also called libscip, though. I strongly believe that you're still linking to the old, incomplete libscip.
Also, your command `cmake GMP=false READLINE=false SHARED=true` makes no sense. Please refer to the [installation instructions for CMake](http://scip.zib.de/doc/html/CMAKE.php). I hope that you're using the SCIP Optimization Suite and not SCIP alone. In this case the installation boils down to this (run from within the extracted SCIPOptSuite directory):

```
mkdir build
cd build
cmake ..
make
make install
```

You should not need to specify the `SCIPOPTDIR` if you properly installed SCIP in this way. You can test the installation by running `scip` in the command line within any directory.

Make sure that you remove any old libraries of SCIP that might still be used for PySCIPOpt before installing SCIP.

Perhaps I didn't make it clear. 

Let's look at baselines' code.  For value function, the loss is `mean(maximum(vf_losses1, vf_losses2))`, which is `mean(maximum(square(vf - R), square(clip(vf) - R)))`. 

For your implementation, the loss of value function is `self.vf_loss = tf.minimum(self.vf_loss1, self.vf_loss2)`, which is `mininum(square(vf - value_target), square(clip(vf) - value_target))`. Note the difference here: you're using `minimum` and Baselines is using `maximum`. 

Probably it's better to take an example. Say, the true value is 0, and our previous value function gives 100 and our current value function gives 50. Assuming the clip range is 10, we've achieved our goal and shouldn't optimize anymore. However, the loss in your code is `minimum((50 - 0)**2, (90 - 0)**2))`, so it will continue pushing 50 to 0. As you can see, using maximum can solve this issue: the maximum is taken on (90 - 0)**2, so there is no gradient on our value function. 

However, your PG loss is the same as Baselines'. 

In Baselines, the PG loss is `pg_losses = mean(maximum(pg_losses, pg_losses2))` where `pg_losses` is like `(-advantage) * ratio` (notice the minus sign here). So overall `pg_losses` equals to `-mean(minimum(advantage * ratio, advantage * clip(ratio)))`. Please also note that their final loss is `loss = pg_loss - entropy * ent_coef + vf_loss * vf_coef`. 

In your code, the PG loss is `self.surr = tf.minimum(self.surr1, self.surr2) = minimum(advantage * ratio, advantage * clip(ratio))`. Your final loss is `-self.surr + ...`, thus PG related term equals to `-minimum(advantage * ratio, advantage * clip(ratio))`, which is the same as Baselines'.

About interface:
The concept of writer exists in many libraries because there you can create a writer and call `write(bytes)` multiple times with different data -- that actually corresponds to "puts" here. That's why I was uncomfortable with the interface. Also, writers can't share the constructor because writers can have their own options and begin() can totally be put in constructor as well. 

To match common terminology of writer, the shared public interface would be `.write(datapoint)` and `.close()` and possibly also a `.dump(dataflow)` which closes itself in the end. 

About function vs class:
It looks nice when they are sharing interfaces but I think `LMDBWriter(filename).dump(dataflow)` may not look as simple as a single function call like `LMDBWriter.dump(filename, dataflow)` or even `LMDBReader.dump(filename, dataflow)`.

I would like to outline another reason to deal with tooltip explicitly - for Labels in particular.  
Let me try to phrase the issue I have encountered.

## Problem: Unwanted tooltip over Label

### Demo

Cf. [demo notebook](http://nbviewer.jupyter.org/urls/gist.githubusercontent.com/oscar6echo/a3244d028968f37898d0231207042730/raw/f05c83a77856f99a1bd8fa0e2ef989fb96e1547a/ipywidget_unfortunate_tooltip_in_description.ipynb) in nbviewer.


+ Run the notebook 3 cells - or see the result on nbviewer (link above)
+ Hover over the text 'My Label' and see the unwanted HTML/CSS :(

### Explanation

The ipywidgets Label, which is part of many other core ipywidgets like XSliders, XText, DropDown, etc is the 'culprit'.  
It passes the description string as HTML label properties `innerHTML` AND `title`. The latter is responsible for the tooltip.  
I would argue that passing the argument to `title` is superfluous at best, and a hindrance when the description contains HTML/CSS that you do not want to show to users - as in the example below.

See source code: https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/2c8c82b0916707d73796a159a6c59f92cea38613/packages/controls/src/widget_description.ts#L75

### Suggested Remedy

1. **Minimum**: Remove the line singled out
1. **Better**: Add new input `tooltip` to widget `Label`. `tooltip` maps to label HTML property `title`.  
If not specified, defaults to `description` i.e. current case.

Ugh, here's a problem with the above: so far I've been trying to keep the `Stream` API a subset of the `socket` API, to keep things simple.

And I think it'd even be reasonable to say that our sockets convert OSErrors into the special trio exceptions in some cases.

But... sometimes sockets aren't streams at all. It'd be really weird to raise `BrokenStreamError` for a UDP `sendto`!

Ideas:

* only do exception type conversion in socket methods if `self.type == SOCK_STREAM`? (Problem: ughh having method signatures change depending on the setting of an attribute really smells bad. Counter-argument: everything about sockets changes depending on the setting of `self.type`, that's just the BSD socket API for you.)

* make the stream view on sockets be a wrapper around the low-level socket object (maybe: the socket API + getsockopt + setsockopt + underlying socket object?), and have the high-level functions like `create_connection` and ... whatever we do for listening ... return a pre-wrapped socket object, so actual sockets become a low-level thing and most people just use the high-level objects?

* do the exception type conversion in any case, but it's OK because UDP sockets never give ECONNRESET or ECONNABORTED? (Problem: is that even true?)

...I guess if we go with the "SocketStream" approach (the 2nd option above) then it might also make sense to move our other weirdness there, like the "improved" default socket options (see #72).

## Packages and modules

### The basics

#### Setup

Create a new directory, call it `melbournescmr`. Now create a subdirectory, call it `melbournep`. Now create two empty python files, one called `melbournem.py`, one called `__init__.py`. Your directory structure should look like this

```
melbournescmr/
    melbournep/
        __init__.py
        melbournem.py
```

#### Explanation

Your top level directory, in this case `melbournescmr`, is the root directory of your source code. If you use version control, this should also be the root of your source code management repository. For example, if you use git, this would be the root directory of your git repository. 

The name of this directory can be whatever you want but generally will be your package name. In this guide we'll call this directory `melbournescmr` to remind us that it's the root of our source code management repository. 

Your package is contained in a subdirectory. This directory contains all your python modules (i.e. scripts) which define the classes, functions, variables etc. etc. used in the package. You can also have subpackages, subsubpackages etc.. In this guide our package is called `melbournep`. The 'p' suffix reminds us that this directory is our package.

The `__init__.py` file tells Python that our `melbournep` directory is a Python package. Its contents are run whenever we `import` our package or one of its submodules/classes so for now let's make its contents:

```python
print "ran melbournep/__init__.py"
```

Your module is a .py file which contains code which defines classes, functions, variables etc. etc. There can be multiple modules in a package and you can have modules in subpackages, subsubpackages etc. The only module we have defined so far is the `melbournem` module. The 'm' suffix reminds us that this is a module. 

Whenever a module is imported, all of the code in it is executed. This is a very important point to keep in mind as it can be very useful. For example, any `import` statements required for your module can be left in the module and don't need to be included in, for example, `__init__.py`. 

For now save `melbournem` with the following contents:

```python
print "Importing melbournem"
print "The whole module is run when we import it."
print "Including any import statements we might have at the top of our module."

def melbournef():
    return("Here's the text that is returned. Melbourne has been voted the "
           "world's most livable city multiple times.")
```

The 'f' suffix reminds us that `melbournef` is a function.

*Note:* All module and package names should be all lowercase and be underscore-separated or have no word-separators at all. If you're going to release your package on PyPI or Conda then the name should be unique too. 

### Executing modules as scripts

When Python modules are run with `python [module].py [arguments]` then their `__name__` is set to `__main__`. Many programs take advantage of this by having the code do something slightly different when it's executed as a script. For example the following code defines a module which provides an add function but also returns the added numbers **only** if it is called with `python add.py [argument 1] [argument 2]`

```python
# add.py
def add(x,y):
    return x + y

if __name == "__main__":
    import sys
    return float(sys.argv[1]) + float(sys.argv[2])

## Importing

### Basics

Packages and modules can be imported in many different ways with different effects. The most useful is importing the desired function directly. 

```python
# import melbournef directly
# the output shows that the __init__.py and melbournem.py files are run
>>> from melbournep.melbournem import melbournef
ran melbournep/__init__.py
Importing melbournem
The whole module is run when we import it.
Including any import statements we might have at the top of our module.

# call it
>>> melbournef()
"Here's the text that is returned. Melbourne has been voted the world's most livable city multiple times."
```

However this **only** works if your working directory contains the `melbournep` directory. If it doesn't then Python can't find the `melbournep` package.

```python
# our current working directory contains the melbournep package
>>> import os
>>> os.listdir('.')
['melbournep']

# so we can import the package
>>> from melbournep.melbournem import melbournef
ran melbournep/__init__.py
Importing melbournem
The whole module is run when we import it.
Including any import statements we might have at the top of our module.
>>> melbournef()
"Here's the text that is returned. Melbourne has been voted the world's most livable city multiple times."
```

```python
# our current working directory doesn't contain the melbournep package
>>> import os
>>> os.listdir('.')
['test.py', 'Animals', 'funniest', 'melbournescmr']

# so we can't import the package
>>> from melbournep import melbournem
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: No module named melbournep
```

### dir()

The built-in `dir()`  function lists the names that we have currently defined whether they be variables, modules, functions, classes, packages etc. It lets you keep track of what you've imported and defined. 

With arguments, `dir([module name])` lists the names that a module has defined. It lets you keep track of the names that can be called from a module with dot notation e.g. `[module].[submodule].[function]`

### Options

Using `dir()`, we can see the effect of importing modules and packages in different ways.

#### Make the function available directly (same as above)

```python
# nothing imported yet
>>> dir()
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__name__', '__package__']

# import our function
>>> from melbournep.melbournem import melbournef
ran melbournep/__init__.py
Importing melbournem
The whole module is run when we import it.
Including any import statements we might have at the top of our module.

# check it was imported
>>> dir()
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__name__', '__package__', 'melbournef']

# the function can be called directly
>>> melbournef()
"Here's the text that is returned. Melbourne has been voted the world's most livable city multiple times."
```

#### Make the module available without its package prefix

```python
# nothing imported yet
>>> dir()
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__name__', '__package__']

# import our module
>>> from melbournep import melbournem
ran melbournep/__init__.py
Importing melbournem
The whole module is run when we import it.
Including any import statements we might have at the top of our module.

# check it was imported
>>> dir()
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__name__', '__package__', 'melbournem']

# check which functions and classes our module defines
>>> dir(melbournem)
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__file__', '__name__', '__package__', 'melbournef']

# to call the function we must use dot notation
>>> melbournem.melbournef()
"Here's the text that is returned. Melbourne has been voted the world's most livable city multiple times."

# the function is not available directly
>>> melbournef()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
NameError: name 'melbournef' is not defined
```

#### Import an individual module from the package

```python
# nothing imported yet
>>> dir()
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__name__', '__package__']

# import our module
>>> import melbournep.melbournem
ran melbournep/__init__.py
Importing melbournem
The whole module is run when we import it.
Including any import statements we might have at the top of our module.

# check it was imported, note that 'melbournep.melbournem' is not explicitly 
# defined, only 'melbournep'
>>> dir()
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__name__', '__package__', 'melbournep']
>>> dir(melbournep)
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__file__', '__name__', '__package__', '__path__', 'melbournem']

# to call the function we must use its full name
>>> melbournep.melbournem.melbournef()
"Here's the text that is returned. Melbourne has been voted the world's most livable city multiple times."

# the module is not available directly
>>> melbournem.melbournef()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
NameError: name 'melbournem' is not defined
```

*Note:* when using `from package import item`, the item can be either a submodule (or subpackage) of the package, or some other name defined in the package, like a function, class or variable. The import statement first tests whether the item is defined in the package; if not, it assumes it is a module and attempts to load it. If it fails to find it, an ImportError exception is raised.

Contrarily, when using syntax like `import item.subitem.subsubitem`, each item except for the last must be a package; the last item can be a module or a package but can’t be a class or function or variable defined in the previous item.
