## \_\_init\_\_.py

### setup

Let's add another function, z2f, to our `melbournem` module by changing it's contents to:

```python
print "Importing melbournem"
print "The whole module is run when we import it."
print "Including any import statements we might have at the top of our module."

def melbournef():
    return("Here's the text that is returned. Melbourne has been voted the "
           "world's most livable city multiple times.")

def z2f():
    return("A different text. Melbourne has 4.5 million inhabitants")
```

Let's also add another module, `z2m`, with contents:

```python
def addf(x,y):
    return (x+y)

def csdf():
    return("Where in the world is Carmen San Diego?")
```

So now our directory structure should look like (you might have some `.pyc` files too, ignore these):

```
melbournescmr/
    melbournep/
        __init__.py
        melbournem.py
        z2m.py
```

### Motivation

If we want to have all the functions in our pacakage available to us we have to do something like

```python
# nothing imported
>>> dir()
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__name__', '__package__']

# import melbournem module
>>> from melbournep import melbournem
ran melbournep/__init__.py
Importing melbournem
The whole module is run when we import it.
Including any import statements we might have at the top of our module.

# check it's there
>>> dir()
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__name__', '__package__', 'melbournem']

# see which submodules, classes and functions it defines
>>> dir(melbournem)
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__file__', '__name__', '__package__',  
 'melbournef', 'z2f']

# import z2m module
>>> from melbournep import z2m

# check it's there
>>> dir()
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__name__', '__package__', 'melbournem', 'z2m']

# see which submodules, classes and functions it defines
>>> dir(z2m)
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__file__', '__name__', '__package__', 
 'addf', 'csdf']

# call some functions 
>>> z2m.csdf()
'Where in the world is Carmen San Diego?'
>>> melbournem.z2f()
'A different text. Melbourne has 4.5 million inhabitants'
```

### Solving with `__init__`.py

Importing each module/function/class from a package individually can be a pain so it is common practice to use the fact that `__init__.py` is run when we import `melbournep` or one of its submodules/subpackages to skip this step. For example, change the contents of `melbournep/__init__.py` to:

```python
print "ran melbournep/__init__.py"

from melbournem import melbournef
from melbournem import z2f

from z2m import addf
from z2m import csdf
```

Now when we import `melbournep` or one of its submodules/subpackages, all the other ones are available to us too through dot notation. 

*Note:* `__init__.py` and the code in our module are only run the first time we import in a session!

```python
# nothing yet imported
>>> dir()
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__name__', '__package__']

# import melbournep
>>> import melbournep
ran melbournep/__init__.py
Importing melbournem
The whole module is run when we import it.
Including any import statements we might have at the top of our module.

# __init__.py has run and melbournep has been imported
>>> dir()
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__name__', '__package__', 'melbournep']

# our __init__.py script makes all our other functions available through dot 
# notation
>>> dir(melbournep)
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__file__', '__name__', '__package__', '__path__', 
 'addf', 'csdf', 'melbournef', 'melbournem', 'z2f', 'z2m']

# try calling some functions
>>> melbournep.z2f()
'A different text. Melbourne has 4.5 million inhabitants'
>>> melbournep.addf(3,6)
9

# importing melbournep again doesn't run __init__.py again!
>>> import melbournep
>>>
# importing melbournem again doesn't run the code in melbournem.py again!
>>> from melbournep import melbournem
>>> 
```

If you want to see a far more complex example of how to use `__init__.py` then have a look at [`__init__.py` on pymagicc github](https://github.com/openclimatedata/pymagicc/blob/master/pymagicc/__ init__.py).

### Sources and further reference

https://www.blog.pythonlibrary.org/2012/07/08/python-201-creating-modules-and-packages/

https://python-packaging.readthedocs.io/en/latest/minimal.html

https://docs.python.org/2/tutorial/modules.html

http://pythoncentral.io/how-to-create-a-python-package/

## Distributing

### An option

As we now know how to make a Python package with modules and functions that are callable from Python, we could just stop here. Unfortunately this would require us to have all the packages, modules and functions we need in our working directory. Cleary this would quickly become a pain. 

To avoid this, various tools have been developed to help make python (and other language) packages distributable and easily installable. 

### setuptools

setuptools is a Python package which is used to easily download, build, install, upgrade, and uninstall Python packages. It seems to be the most widely used and best package for this purpose so it's what I use. 

#### Installing setuptools

If you haven't yet, make sure you have installed pip. Instructions: [https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/installing/](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/installing/)

To install setuptools run the following in terminal

```
pip install -U pip setuptools
``` 

#### `setup.py`

This file acts as the main entrypoint to register the package name on PyPI and upload source distributions. It contains a single call to setuptools so it can also be used to install the package locally.

##### Basics

In the root of your source code management repository create a file called `setup.py` with the following contents.

```python
from setuptools import setup

setup(name='melbournen',
      version='0.1.0',
      description='Melbourne examples',
      author='City of Melbourne',
      author_email='mel@example.com',
      packages=['melbournep'],
      zip_safe=False)
```

I have used `melbournen` for the name to highlight that this is just the name that is used by setuptools and doesn't actually affect the package or module name. Your directory should now have the following structure.

```
melbournescmr/
    melbournep/
        __init__.py
        melbournem.py
        z2m.py
    setup.py
```

We can now install the package locally into the active environment/for our default version of Python by running the following command whilst in the directory containing `setup.py`

```
pip install [path to directory containing setup.py]
```

Hence in our case we run

```
pip install melbournescmr/
```

The output should look something like 

```
Processing ./melbournescmr
Installing collected packages: melbournen
  Running setup.py install for melbournen ... done
Successfully installed melbournen-0.1.0
```

We can check that our package was installed using `pip list`. The output should be something like

```
Package        Version Location
-------------- ------- ----------------------------
...   
melbournen     0.1.0
...
```

We can now import our modules, packages and functions in Python, irrespective of our current working directory.

```python
# We're in a directory that doesn't contain our package
>>> import os
>>> os.listdir('.')
['something.txt']

# We can import the package defined in setup.py and then call functions
>>> import melbournep
ran melbournep/__init__.py
Importing melbournem
The whole module is run when we import it.
Including any import statements we might have at the top of our module.

>>> dir()
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__name__', '__package__', 'melbournep', 'os']
>>> dir(melbournep)
['__builtins__', '__doc__', '__file__', '__name__', '__package__', '__path__', 
 'addf', 'csdf', 'melbournef', 'melbournem', 'z2f', 'z2m']
>>> melbournep.melbournef()
"Here's the text that is returned. Melbourne has been voted the world's most livable city multiple times."

# However the name, melbournen, doesn't define a package so can't be imported
>>> import melbournen
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: No module named melbournen
```

The fact that the `name` field in `setup.py` doesn't actually let you import the package is why most people tend to make `name` and `packages` the same. If you want to break a big package down into more manageable bits then you can use subpackages which can be called with dot notation i.e. `from [package].[subpackage] import function`

Finally we can uninstall the package using 

```
pip uninstall melbournen
```

Answer 'y' to the question to proceed with uninstall

```
pip uninstall melbournen
Uninstalling melbournen-0.1.0:
  .../miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/melbournen-0.1.0-py2.7.egg-info
  .../miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/melbournep/__init__.py
  .../miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/melbournep/__init__.pyc
  .../miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/melbournep/melbournem.py
  .../miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/melbournep/melbournem.pyc
  .../miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/melbournep/z2m.py
  .../miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/melbournep/z2m.pyc
Proceed (y/n)? y
  Successfully uninstalled melbournen-0.1.0
```

##### README

In the same directory as `setup.py`, add a `README` file as well as a file called `MANIFEST.in`. For this exercise we will use markdown for our `README` file so your scmr should now look like 

```
melbournescmr/
    melbournep/
        __init__.py
        melbournem.py
        z2m.py
    setup.py
    README.md
    MANIFEST.in
```

Obviously you can use whatever file format you want for your `README` file, common ones are `.txt`, `.rst` and `.md`.

To tell setuptools to include `README.md` when generating source distributions, `MANIFEST.in` should contain 

```
include README.[extension]
```

which in our case would be 

```
include README.md
```

You must also add the `include_package_data=True` argument to `setup.py`

```python
setup(
      ...
      include_package_data=True,
      ...
      )
```

##### Other fields

###### Specifying dependencies

In `setup.py` add an `install_requires` argument. The value of `install_requires` should be a list like so

```python
setup(
      ...
      install_requires=['numpy',
                        'scipy'],
      ...
      )
```

Each dependency must match the name of the file on PyPI. (You can also specify packages which aren’t on PyPI with some massaging but we shouldn't need this for a while.)

Now when we install `melbournen` we can see that `setuptools` also checks for the dependencies.

```
$ pip install melbournescmr/
Processing ./melbournescmr
Requirement already satisfied: numpy in .../miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages (from melbournen==0.1.0)
Requirement already satisfied: scipy in .../miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages (from melbournen==0.1.0)
Installing collected packages: melbournen
  Running setup.py install for melbournen ... done
Successfully installed melbournen-0.1.0
```

###### Long description

We should also include a longer description in our `setup.py` file. We can do this with the `long_description` keyword. 

```python
setup(
      ...
      long_description="We can put some longer description in here.",
      ...
      )
```

If you have a `README` then a little bit of code to read it in keeps things all in one place.

```python
def readme():
    with open('[name of README file with extension]') as f:
        return f.read()

setup(
      ...
      long_description=readme(),
      ...
      )
```

###### Keywords

In `setup.py` add a `keywords` argument. It should be a space separated list of keywords e.g.

```python
setup(
      ...
      keywords = "melbourne example boasting",
      ...
      )
```

###### url

In `setup.py` add a `url` argument. It should be a link to an online repository where your code is hosted.

```python
setup(
      ...
      url="http://melbourneisthebest.com/code",
      ...
      )
```

###### Tests

Place tests in a submodule of the directory containing your package i.e.

```
melbournescmr/
    melbournep/
            tests/
                __init__.py
                test_melbournef.py
        __init__.py
        melbournem.py
        z2m.py
    setup.py
    README.md
    MANIFEST.in
```

In `test_melbournef.py` we use a `unittest.TestCase` subclass which is overkill for now but provides good infrastructure for later development.

```
from unittest import TestCase

import melbournep

class TestMelbournef(TestCase):
    def test_is_string(self):
        s = melbournep.melbournef()
        self.assertTrue(isinstance(s, basestring))
```

To use the tests we should then install `nose`

```
pip install nose
```

The tests can then be run by calling `nosetests` in terminal from the scmr. 

```
$ pwd
.../melbournescmr
$ nosetests
.
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 0.074s

OK
```

To integrate these tests into `setup.py` add the following lines

```python
setup(
    ...
    test_suite='nose.collector',
    tests_require=['nose'],
    ...
)
```

so that our tests can simply be run by calling `python setup.py test` from our scmr, with output something like

```
$ pwd
.../melbournescmr
$ python setup.py test
running test
running egg_info
creating melbournen.egg-info
writing requirements to melbournen.egg-info/requires.txt
writing melbournen.egg-info/PKG-INFO
writing top-level names to melbournen.egg-info/top_level.txt
writing dependency_links to melbournen.egg-info/dependency_links.txt
writing manifest file 'melbournen.egg-info/SOURCES.txt'
reading manifest file 'melbournen.egg-info/SOURCES.txt'
reading manifest template 'MANIFEST.in'
writing manifest file 'melbournen.egg-info/SOURCES.txt'
running build_ext
test_is_string (melbournep.tests.test_melbournef.TestMelbournef) ... ok

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 0.006s

OK
```

###### Other classifiers

Better package metadata can be added with the `classifiers` keyword alongside some others. For a full list of arguments to `classifiers` visit http://pypi.python.org/pypi?%3Aaction=list_classifiers.

#### Source distributions and wheels

##### Create source distribution

To create the source distribution, from the scmr directory simply run

```
$ pwd
.../melbournescmr
$ python setup.py sdist
```

This creates `melbournescmr/dist/[package name]-[version].tar.gz` with terminal output something like: 

(Ignore the warnings. Using `.md` format for `README` is fine and we don't have a url for our package.)

##### `.gitignore`

Once you've created your source distribution and wheel you should make sure that you have a well-configured `.gitignore` or similar file. This prevents compiled distributions from appearing in your source code repository, something you don't want. 

```
# Compiled python modules.
*.pyc

# Setuptools distribution folder.
/dist/

# Python egg metadata, regenerated from source files by setuptools.
/*.egg-info
/*.egg

# Build folder
/build
```

##### Installing with source distribution or wheel

Other users can then easily install the package by either of the `.tar.gz` or `.whl` files and running any of

```
pip install [project.whl file]
pip install [project.tar.gz file]
``` 
#### Sources and further reference

https://python-packaging.readthedocs.io/en/latest/minimal.html

https://packaging.python.org/distributing/

### PiPY and pip

Before you start, install `twine`, the package we'll use to communicate with the PyPI repository, by running the following in terminal

```
pip install twine
```

#### Registering with PyPI

If you haven't already done so, create a [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi) and a [TestPyPI](https://testpypi.python.org/pypi) account manually online. Don’t create an account through `python setup.py register` as there are security concerns.

To help you interact with the PyPI repositories, create a file in your home directory called `.pypirc` with contents

```
[distutils]
index-servers=
    pypi
    testpypi

[testpypi]
repository = https://testpypi.python.org/pypi
username = [username]

[pypi]
repository = https://pypi.python.org/pypi
username = [username]
```

#### Registering your package

To register the package with PyPI i.e. reserve the name, upload package metadata, and create the pypi.python.org webpage: 

`twine register dist/[pack-name-version].whl`

To register with the test site use:

`twine register dist/[pack-name-version].whl -r testpypi`

If you're not asked for your password then seomthing weird is going on.

#### Upload to PyPI

From the scmr directory simply run

`twine upload dist/* -r testpypi`

If you're not asked for your password then seomthing weird is going on.

#### Test the install

Once the package is uploaded, other users can install it with 

```
pip install [package name]
pip install -i https://testpypi.python.org/pypi [package name]
```

#### Sources and further reference

https://packaging.python.org/distributing/

https://python-packaging.readthedocs.io/en/latest/minimal.html

More detail on setup can can be found with `python setup.py --help-commands`

You can also do stuff with command line scripts, adding non-code files etc. which I haven't got to. 

### Conda

{Follow up} Also distributable with Conda. Note that Conda can be extended to handle more than just python, unlike pip. Easy way seems to be to follow https://conda.io/docs/build_tutorials/pkgs.html once you have a package on PyPI.

There is some movement within Python to make the GIL [a per interpreter lock](https://www.blog.pythonlibrary.org/2018/04/02/pydev-of-the-week-eric-snow/) which would allow sub-interpreters to run with separate locks. These changes are starting with [PEP-554,](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0554/) so I would recommend anyone who wants this functionality should support that PEP. I do not think we can ever resolve this issue without these types of changes in Python.

However, I am worry about the network API, and suggest to support two ways at the same time.

- Existing way compiles the network in real-time.

```python
net = InputLayer(net)
net = DenseLayer(net, 100)
```

- Keras way compiles the network in the end.

```python
net = InputLayer()(net)
net = DenseLayer(100)(net)
net.compile()
```

In my opinion, the main differences between our way and keras way is they compile the network in the end but we simply compile network in real-time, and we mix up layer and network together.

While, to build complex network, in some cases, the next layer will need the information (like shape) of the input or outputs of previous layers. But the input and `net.outputs` is unknown before we compile it. Take dynamic RNN layer below for example, we need the input placeholder/tensor to compute the `sequence_length`. Therefore, if we compile the network in the end, we can't give the `sequence_length` to `DynamicRNNLayer` .

```python
net = EmbeddingInputlayer(
                     inputs = x,
                     vocabulary_size = 1000,
                     embedding_size = 200,
                     name = 'ebb')
net = DynamicRNNLayer(net,
                     cell_fn = LSTMCell,
                     n_hidden = 200,
                     dropout = (keep_prob if is_train else None),
                     sequence_length = tl.layers.retrieve_seq_length_op2(x),
                     return_last = True,
                     name = 'ann')
```


Let me discuss with more users, I will put more feedback later.

> Further, I have no valid cell barcodes available to me from the SRA sample FASTQs (short read archive). I don't think that will be a problem, even though you warn against this.

``SmartSeq2`` Logic would be the more appropriate.
